title: Geometry of the Gaussian Graphical Model of the Cycle
speaker: Rodica Dinu
abstract:
Algebraic statistics employs techniques in algebraic geometry, commutative algebra and combinatorics, to address problems in statistics and its applications. The philosophy of algebraic statistics is that statistical models are algebraic varieties. We will discuss two conjectures on the geometry of the Gaussian graphical model of the cycle. One conjecture is due to Sturmfels and Uhler concerning the degree of the projective variety associated to the Gaussian graphical model of the cycle. We present a new approach based on the intersection theory of the variety of complete quadrics, due to Michalek, Monin, and Wisniewski. The other conjecture is due to Drton, Sturmfels and Sullivant concerning the maximum likelihood degree of the same model.
This talk is based on joint works with Carlos Amendola, Mateusz Michalek and Martin Vodicka.

proof_stages.label: Geometric encoding of likelihood invariants
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines linear concentration models, their degree and ML degree, and explains likelihood maximization geometrically as a projection/fiber problem, with an additional determinant-gradient/multidegree interpretation.

proof_stages.label: Cycle graphical model and target conjectures
proof_stages.summary: Gaussian graphical models are introduced by zero patterns in the concentration matrix, then the two conjectural formulas for the degree and ML degree of the n-cycle model are stated as the main targets.

proof_stages.label: Complete quadrics as the intersection-theoretic ambient space
proof_stages.summary: After explaining why naive intersection theory in symmetric matrices gives wrong enumerative counts, the proof moves to the compactification by complete quadrics, describes it via minors and successive blow-ups, and identifies the cycle degree with an intersection of the strict transform with a power of the divisor L_{n-1}.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of the degree computation to two boundary intersections
proof_stages.summary: Using Picard/cohomology relations among the divisors L_i and boundary classes S_i, the desired intersection is expanded so that all but two types of terms vanish, and those remaining terms are computed to obtain the conjectured degree formula.

proof_stages.label: ML degree as a chosen transverse fiber
proof_stages.summary: The ML degree of the cycle model is reformulated as the number of points in a suitable affine-linear intersection, provided transversality and avoidance of the base locus hold, and the identity matrix is chosen as the tractable fiber.

proof_stages.label: Sign symmetry and singular fiber analysis
proof_stages.summary: The identity fiber is shown to be invariant under conjugation by the diagonal sign group, and the singular matrices in the relevant intersection are classified by parity of n, with checkerboard-type matrices appearing only for even n.

proof_stages.label: Explicit fiber parametrization and orbit count
proof_stages.summary: The remaining fiber points are described by explicit matrix families governed by determinant recurrence polynomials, whose roots and sign-group orbits give exactly the conjectured ML-degree formula after smoothness/transversality verification.

examples.name: Chasles’ conic-counting problem
examples.note: The classical count of smooth conics tangent to five given conics shows that naive Bézout intersection in the ambient space counts degenerate double lines, motivating the move to complete quadrics.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies a Bézout count to the five-conics problem, computes the naive 6^5 count, and explains why it overcounts before citing 3264

examples.name: Matroidal reciprocal-linear-space analogy
examples.note: The analogy with matroidal linear spaces inside diagonal matrices and the permutohedral blow-up is presented as an inspiration for using complete quadrics for symmetric matrices.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only invokes the reciprocal-linear-space/matroid setting as an analogy and source of inspiration for complete quadrics

examples.name: Small cycle computations
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions computations for the 4-, 5-, and possibly 6-cycle as small cases that may have suggested the conjectural formulas, without deriving any new idea from them.
examples.engagement_reason: small cycles such as 4-, 5-, and 6-cycles are only cited as computer-checkable cases behind the conjecture, without carrying out those computations

passes.reason: Computations for small cycles are ordinary initial cases used as evidence or motivation for conjectured formulas, not structural exceptions or counterexamples.
